scenarioMeta.name: 1991 India Market Opening
scenarioMeta.description: End of 'License Raj' and opening of the Indian economy.

sectors.name: Technology
sectors.description: IT services began their global ascent.

sectors.name: Automotive
sectors.description: Legacy manufacturers facing new global competition.

sectors.name: Banking
sectors.description: Private banks emerged to challenge state monopolies.

sectors.name: Consumer
sectors.description: A new middle class demanded global brands.

sectors.name: Commodities
sectors.description: Steel and cement grew with the economy.

news.title: SEBI Granted Statutory Powers
news.summary: The Securities and Exchange Board of India becomes an autonomous body.

news.title: Foreign Direct Investment Rules Relaxed
news.summary: Government allows up to 51% foreign equity in high-priority industries.

news.title: Inflation Remains Stubbornly High
news.summary: Prices continue to rise despite monetary tightening.

news.title: Sensex Crosses 2,000 Mark
news.summary: Stock market rallies on liberalization hopes.

news.title: Manmohan Singh Presents Historic Budget
news.summary: Import duties slashed, rupee made partially convertible.

news.title: FIIs Allowed to Invest
news.summary: Foreign Institutional Investors can now enter the Indian stock market.

news.title: Market Frenzy Continues
news.summary: Retail investors flock to Dalal Street as indices soar.

news.title: Trade Deficit Narrows
news.summary: Devaluation of the rupee begins to boost exports.

news.title: Sensex Hits 4,000
news.summary: The index doubles in just a few months. 'Big Bull' Harshad Mehta leads the charge.

news.title: Banks Report Record Profits
news.summary: Financial sector benefits from market boom and high interest rates.

news.title: Concerns Over Market Valuation
news.summary: Some analysts warn that the rally is disconnected from economic fundamentals.

news.title: Rupee Stabilizes
news.summary: Currency markets calm down after initial volatility post-convertibility.

news.title: The Scam Breaks
news.summary: Journalist Sucheta Dalal exposes a massive ₹4,000 crore securities scam involving Harshad Mehta.

news.title: Market Crashes
news.summary: Sensex plummets as panic selling grips Dalal Street.

news.title: Banks Implicated
news.summary: Several major banks found to have issued fake bank receipts.

news.title: RBI Launches Investigation
news.summary: Central bank steps in to audit the banking system's exposure.

news.title: Harshad Mehta Arrested
news.summary: CBI takes the 'Big Bull' into custody.

news.title: Trading Halted
news.summary: BSE shuts down temporarily due to payment crisis and broker defaults.

news.title: Foreign Investors Spooked
news.summary: FIIs pause investments amid regulatory uncertainty.

news.title: Industrial Production Picks Up
news.summary: Real economy shows signs of life despite financial market turmoil.

news.title: SEBI Tightens Regulations
news.summary: New rules introduced to prevent a repeat of the securities scam.

news.title: Market Begins Slow Recovery
news.summary: Bargain hunters step in as valuations become attractive again.

news.title: Monsoon Arrives on Time
news.summary: Good rains boost agricultural outlook and rural demand.

news.title: Software Exports Surge
news.summary: IT companies report strong dollar earnings.

investorDecisions.name: Warren Buffett
investorDecisions.philosophy: Value investing, long horizon, avoids leverage

investorDecisions.name: George Soros
investorDecisions.philosophy: Macro bets, reflexivity theory, high conviction short positions

investorDecisions.name: Peter Lynch
investorDecisions.philosophy: Growth at reasonable price, retail insights, sector rotation

investorDecisions.name: Rakesh Jhunjhunwala
investorDecisions.philosophy: India growth story, concentrated bets, high risk

legendaryQuotes.quote: Price is what you pay. Value is what you get.
Markets are constantly in a state of uncertainty and flux, and money is made by discounting the obvious and betting on the unexpected.
In this business, if you're good, you're right six times out of ten. You're never going to be right nine times out of ten.
Respect the market. Have an open mind. Know what to stake. Know when to take a loss. Be responsible.
